instruction: How do I build complex queries with Diesel's query builder including joins, aggregations, subqueries, and dynamic filtering?
output:
<think>
Diesel's query builder is type-safe at compile time, which means queries that wouldn't work are caught before runtime. The key patterns: 1) Joins using `.inner_join()` / `.left_join()` with the `belongs_to` / `associations` macro, 2) Aggregations with `diesel::dsl::count`, `sum`, `avg` etc., 3) Subqueries via `.filter(column.eq_any(subquery))`, 4) Dynamic filtering using `BoxedExpression` or `.into_boxed()` for queries built at runtime. For joins, Diesel requires explicit `allow_tables_to_appear_in_same_query!` and `joinable!` macros. The `QueryDsl` trait provides the fluent API. For dynamic queries, `.into_boxed()` converts a typed query into a `BoxedSelectStatement` that can be conditionally modified. Important: Diesel 2.x changed the group_by API — you must explicitly specify grouped columns. Custom SQL fragments use `sql_literal` or `sql::<Type>("...")`. I'll show practical examples of each pattern with proper type annotations.
</think>

Here's a comprehensive Diesel query builder guide:

```rust
use diesel::prelude::*;
use diesel::dsl::{count, sum, avg, max, now};
use diesel::sql_types::{Integer, Text, BigInt};

// === Schema (generated by diesel CLI) ===
table! {
    users (id) {
        id -> Integer,
        name -> Text,
        email -> Text,
        role -> Text,
        created_at -> Timestamp,
    }
}

table! {
    orders (id) {
        id -> Integer,
        user_id -> Integer,
        product -> Text,
        amount_cents -> Integer,
        status -> Text,
        created_at -> Timestamp,
    }
}

table! {
    order_items (id) {
        id -> Integer,
        order_id -> Integer,
        product_name -> Text,
        quantity -> Integer,
        unit_price_cents -> Integer,
    }
}

joinable!(orders -> users (user_id));
joinable!(order_items -> orders (order_id));
allow_tables_to_appear_in_same_query!(users, orders, order_items);

// === Models ===
#[derive(Queryable, Selectable, Debug)]
#[diesel(table_name = users)]
struct User {
    id: i32,
    name: String,
    email: String,
    role: String,
    created_at: chrono::NaiveDateTime,
}

#[derive(Queryable, Selectable, Associations, Debug)]
#[diesel(belongs_to(User))]
#[diesel(table_name = orders)]
struct Order {
    id: i32,
    user_id: i32,
    product: String,
    amount_cents: i32,
    status: String,
    created_at: chrono::NaiveDateTime,
}

// === Query patterns ===
fn join_queries(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> QueryResult<()> {
    // Inner join: users with their orders
    let results: Vec<(User, Order)> = users::table
        .inner_join(orders::table)
        .select((User::as_select(), Order::as_select()))
        .load(conn)?;

    // Left join: all users, optionally with orders
    let results: Vec<(User, Option<Order>)> = users::table
        .left_join(orders::table)
        .select((User::as_select(), Order::as_select().nullable()))
        .load(conn)?;

    // Multi-table join
    let results: Vec<(String, String, i32)> = users::table
        .inner_join(orders::table.inner_join(order_items::table))
        .select((
            users::name,
            order_items::product_name,
            order_items::quantity,
        ))
        .load(conn)?;

    Ok(())
}

// === Aggregations ===
#[derive(QueryableByName, Debug)]
struct UserStats {
    #[diesel(sql_type = Text)]
    name: String,
    #[diesel(sql_type = BigInt)]
    order_count: i64,
    #[diesel(sql_type = BigInt)]
    total_spent: i64,
}

fn aggregation_queries(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> QueryResult<()> {
    // Count orders per user
    let results: Vec<(String, i64)> = users::table
        .inner_join(orders::table)
        .group_by(users::name)
        .select((users::name, count(orders::id)))
        .load(conn)?;

    // Sum with filtering
    let total: Option<i64> = orders::table
        .filter(orders::status.eq("completed"))
        .select(sum(orders::amount_cents))
        .first(conn)?;

    // Complex aggregation via raw SQL
    let stats: Vec<UserStats> = diesel::sql_query(
        "SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id) as order_count, \
         COALESCE(SUM(o.amount_cents), 0) as total_spent \
         FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id \
         GROUP BY u.name ORDER BY total_spent DESC"
    ).load(conn)?;

    Ok(())
}

// === Subqueries ===
fn subquery_patterns(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> QueryResult<()> {
    // Users who have placed orders > $100
    let high_spender_ids = orders::table
        .filter(orders::amount_cents.gt(10000))
        .select(orders::user_id);

    let high_spenders: Vec<User> = users::table
        .filter(users::id.eq_any(high_spender_ids))
        .select(User::as_select())
        .load(conn)?;

    // Users who have NOT placed any orders
    let users_with_orders = orders::table.select(orders::user_id);
    let inactive: Vec<User> = users::table
        .filter(diesel::dsl::not(users::id.eq_any(users_with_orders)))
        .select(User::as_select())
        .load(conn)?;

    Ok(())
}

// === Dynamic filtering (runtime query building) ===
struct OrderFilter {
    status: Option<String>,
    min_amount: Option<i32>,
    max_amount: Option<i32>,
    user_id: Option<i32>,
    product_search: Option<String>,
}

fn dynamic_query(
    conn: &mut PgConnection,
    filter: &OrderFilter,
) -> QueryResult<Vec<Order>> {
    let mut query = orders::table
        .select(Order::as_select())
        .into_boxed();  // Enables dynamic modification

    if let Some(ref status) = filter.status {
        query = query.filter(orders::status.eq(status));
    }
    if let Some(min) = filter.min_amount {
        query = query.filter(orders::amount_cents.ge(min));
    }
    if let Some(max) = filter.max_amount {
        query = query.filter(orders::amount_cents.le(max));
    }
    if let Some(uid) = filter.user_id {
        query = query.filter(orders::user_id.eq(uid));
    }
    if let Some(ref search) = filter.product_search {
        query = query.filter(orders::product.ilike(format!("%{}%", search)));
    }

    query.order(orders::created_at.desc()).load(conn)
}
```

instruction: How do I implement custom SQL types in Diesel for handling PostgreSQL enums, JSONB columns, and domain-specific types?
output:
<think>
Diesel's type system maps Rust types to SQL types. For custom types: 1) PostgreSQL enums need both a SQL type definition and Rust enum with `ToSql`/`FromSql` impls, 2) JSONB columns can be mapped to serde types using `diesel::sql_types::Jsonb`, 3) Domain types (like Money, Email) wrap primitive types with validation. The key traits: `ToSql<SqlType, Backend>` for Rust->SQL conversion, `FromSql<SqlType, Backend>` for SQL->Rust, `AsExpression` for use in query builder expressions, `FromSqlRow` for reading from result sets. Diesel 2.x uses `#[derive(diesel::AsExpression, diesel::FromSqlRow)]` for simpler custom types. For PG enums, `diesel::deserialize::FromSql` reads the text representation from the wire protocol. I need to show: a PG enum mapped to a Rust enum, a JSONB column mapped to a serde struct, and a newtype wrapper with validation. The `#[diesel(sql_type = ...)]` attribute links the Rust type to its SQL representation.
</think>

Here's how to implement custom types in Diesel:

```rust
use diesel::prelude::*;
use diesel::pg::{Pg, PgValue};
use diesel::deserialize::{self, FromSql, FromSqlRow};
use diesel::serialize::{self, IsNull, Output, ToSql};
use diesel::sql_types::{Text, Integer, Jsonb};
use diesel::expression::AsExpression;
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
use std::io::Write;

// ============================================
// 1. PostgreSQL Enum Type
// ============================================

// SQL: CREATE TYPE order_status AS ENUM ('pending', 'processing', 'shipped', 'delivered', 'cancelled');
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, AsExpression, FromSqlRow)]
#[diesel(sql_type = diesel::sql_types::Text)]  // PG enums transfer as text
pub enum OrderStatus {
    Pending,
    Processing,
    Shipped,
    Delivered,
    Cancelled,
}

impl ToSql<Text, Pg> for OrderStatus {
    fn to_sql<'b>(&'b self, out: &mut Output<'b, '_, Pg>) -> serialize::Result {
        let s = match self {
            OrderStatus::Pending => "pending",
            OrderStatus::Processing => "processing",
            OrderStatus::Shipped => "shipped",
            OrderStatus::Delivered => "delivered",
            OrderStatus::Cancelled => "cancelled",
        };
        out.write_all(s.as_bytes())?;
        Ok(IsNull::No)
    }
}

impl FromSql<Text, Pg> for OrderStatus {
    fn from_sql(bytes: PgValue<'_>) -> deserialize::Result<Self> {
        match bytes.as_bytes() {
            b"pending" => Ok(OrderStatus::Pending),
            b"processing" => Ok(OrderStatus::Processing),
            b"shipped" => Ok(OrderStatus::Shipped),
            b"delivered" => Ok(OrderStatus::Delivered),
            b"cancelled" => Ok(OrderStatus::Cancelled),
            other => Err(format!(
                "Unknown order status: {}",
                String::from_utf8_lossy(other)
            ).into()),
        }
    }
}

// ============================================
// 2. JSONB Column -> Serde Struct
// ============================================
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize, AsExpression, FromSqlRow)]
#[diesel(sql_type = Jsonb)]
pub struct UserPreferences {
    pub theme: String,
    pub notifications_enabled: bool,
    pub language: String,
    pub timezone: String,
    #[serde(default)]
    pub custom_fields: std::collections::HashMap<String, String>,
}

impl ToSql<Jsonb, Pg> for UserPreferences {
    fn to_sql<'b>(&'b self, out: &mut Output<'b, '_, Pg>) -> serialize::Result {
        let json = serde_json::to_value(self)?;
        <serde_json::Value as ToSql<Jsonb, Pg>>::to_sql(&json, &mut out.reborrow())
    }
}

impl FromSql<Jsonb, Pg> for UserPreferences {
    fn from_sql(bytes: PgValue<'_>) -> deserialize::Result<Self> {
        let json = <serde_json::Value as FromSql<Jsonb, Pg>>::from_sql(bytes)?;
        Ok(serde_json::from_value(json)?)
    }
}

// ============================================
// 3. Domain Type: validated Email
// ============================================
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, AsExpression, FromSqlRow)]
#[diesel(sql_type = Text)]
pub struct Email(String);

impl Email {
    pub fn new(value: &str) -> Result<Self, String> {
        if !value.contains('@') || value.len() < 3 {
            return Err(format!("Invalid email: {}", value));
        }
        Ok(Email(value.to_lowercase()))
    }

    pub fn as_str(&self) -> &str {
        &self.0
    }

    pub fn domain(&self) -> &str {
        self.0.split('@').nth(1).unwrap_or("")
    }
}

impl ToSql<Text, Pg> for Email {
    fn to_sql<'b>(&'b self, out: &mut Output<'b, '_, Pg>) -> serialize::Result {
        out.write_all(self.0.as_bytes())?;
        Ok(IsNull::No)
    }
}

impl FromSql<Text, Pg> for Email {
    fn from_sql(bytes: PgValue<'_>) -> deserialize::Result<Self> {
        let s = <String as FromSql<Text, Pg>>::from_sql(bytes)?;
        Email::new(&s).map_err(|e| e.into())
    }
}

// ============================================
// 4. Domain Type: Money (cents storage)
// ============================================
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, AsExpression, FromSqlRow)]
#[diesel(sql_type = Integer)]
pub struct Money(i32);  // Stored as cents

impl Money {
    pub fn from_dollars(dollars: f64) -> Self {
        Money((dollars * 100.0).round() as i32)
    }

    pub fn cents(&self) -> i32 { self.0 }
    pub fn dollars(&self) -> f64 { self.0 as f64 / 100.0 }

    pub fn add(&self, other: Money) -> Money {
        Money(self.0 + other.0)
    }
}

impl ToSql<Integer, Pg> for Money {
    fn to_sql<'b>(&'b self, out: &mut Output<'b, '_, Pg>) -> serialize::Result {
        <i32 as ToSql<Integer, Pg>>::to_sql(&self.0, &mut out.reborrow())
    }
}

impl FromSql<Integer, Pg> for Money {
    fn from_sql(bytes: PgValue<'_>) -> deserialize::Result<Self> {
        Ok(Money(<i32 as FromSql<Integer, Pg>>::from_sql(bytes)?))
    }
}

// ============================================
// 5. Model using custom types
// ============================================
table! {
    orders (id) {
        id -> Integer,
        user_email -> Text,      // Maps to Email
        status -> Text,          // Maps to OrderStatus
        total -> Integer,        // Maps to Money
        preferences -> Jsonb,    // Maps to UserPreferences
    }
}

#[derive(Queryable, Insertable, Debug)]
#[diesel(table_name = orders)]
struct OrderRow {
    id: i32,
    user_email: Email,
    status: OrderStatus,
    total: Money,
    preferences: UserPreferences,
}

fn query_example(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> QueryResult<Vec<OrderRow>> {
    use self::orders::dsl::*;

    // Custom types work in filters
    orders
        .filter(status.eq(OrderStatus::Shipped))
        .filter(total.gt(Money::from_dollars(50.0)))
        .load(conn)
}
```

instruction: How do I set up Diesel migrations properly with rollback support, seed data, and testing strategies for schema management?
output:
<think>
Diesel migrations are SQL files managed by the `diesel` CLI tool. Each migration has an `up.sql` and `down.sql` file. Key practices: 1) Migrations must be idempotent where possible (use `IF NOT EXISTS`), 2) Down migrations should perfectly reverse the up, 3) Seed data should be separate from schema migrations, 4) Testing should use a fresh database per test or transactions that roll back. The `diesel migration run` applies pending migrations, `diesel migration redo` tests rollback by running down then up. For CI, `diesel migration pending` checks if migrations need running. For testing, the `diesel_migrations` crate provides `run_pending_migrations` to set up test databases programmatically. Best practice: each migration should be atomic and self-contained. For seed data, create a separate migration or a Rust function that runs after migrations. For testing, use `conn.begin_test_transaction()` which wraps each test in a transaction that rolls back automatically. I should show the file structure, migration examples, the embed_migrations macro, and test helpers.
</think>

Here's a comprehensive Diesel migrations and testing setup:

```rust
// === Project structure ===
// migrations/
//   2024-01-01-000000_create_users/
//     up.sql
//     down.sql
//   2024-01-01-000001_create_orders/
//     up.sql
//     down.sql
//   2024-01-01-000002_add_user_indexes/
//     up.sql
//     down.sql
//   2024-01-01-000003_seed_data/
//     up.sql
//     down.sql

// --- migrations/2024-01-01-000000_create_users/up.sql ---
// CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
//     id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
//     name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
//     email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
//     role VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'user',
//     password_hash VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
//     created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
//     updated_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
//     deleted_at TIMESTAMP
// );
//
// CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
// CREATE INDEX idx_users_role ON users(role);

// --- migrations/2024-01-01-000000_create_users/down.sql ---
// DROP TABLE IF EXISTS users;

// --- migrations/2024-01-01-000001_create_orders/up.sql ---
// CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
//     id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
//     user_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
//     status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
//     total_cents INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
//     metadata JSONB DEFAULT '{}',
//     created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
// );
//
// CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id);
// CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

// --- migrations/2024-01-01-000003_seed_data/up.sql ---
// INSERT INTO users (name, email, role, password_hash)
// VALUES ('Admin', 'admin@example.com', 'admin', 'hash_placeholder')
// ON CONFLICT (email) DO NOTHING;

// === Rust: Embed and run migrations ===
use diesel_migrations::{embed_migrations, EmbeddedMigrations, MigrationHarness};
use diesel::pg::PgConnection;
use diesel::prelude::*;

pub const MIGRATIONS: EmbeddedMigrations = embed_migrations!("migrations");

pub fn run_migrations(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    conn.run_pending_migrations(MIGRATIONS)?;
    Ok(())
}

pub fn check_pending(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> bool {
    conn.has_pending_migration(MIGRATIONS).unwrap_or(true)
}

pub fn revert_last(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    conn.revert_last_migration(MIGRATIONS)?;
    Ok(())
}

// === Database pool setup ===
use diesel::r2d2::{self, ConnectionManager, Pool};

pub type DbPool = Pool<ConnectionManager<PgConnection>>;

pub fn create_pool(database_url: &str) -> DbPool {
    let manager = ConnectionManager::<PgConnection>::new(database_url);
    Pool::builder()
        .max_size(10)
        .min_idle(Some(2))
        .test_on_check_out(true)
        .build(manager)
        .expect("Failed to create pool")
}

pub fn init_db(pool: &DbPool) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    let mut conn = pool.get()?;
    run_migrations(&mut conn)?;
    Ok(())
}

// === Programmatic seed data ===
pub fn seed_development_data(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> QueryResult<()> {
    use crate::schema::users;

    #[derive(Insertable)]
    #[diesel(table_name = users)]
    struct NewUser<'a> {
        name: &'a str,
        email: &'a str,
        role: &'a str,
        password_hash: &'a str,
    }

    let seeds = vec![
        NewUser { name: "Alice", email: "alice@test.com", role: "admin", password_hash: "hash1" },
        NewUser { name: "Bob", email: "bob@test.com", role: "user", password_hash: "hash2" },
        NewUser { name: "Charlie", email: "charlie@test.com", role: "user", password_hash: "hash3" },
    ];

    diesel::insert_into(users::table)
        .values(&seeds)
        .on_conflict_do_nothing()
        .execute(conn)?;

    Ok(())
}

// === Testing helpers ===
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use diesel::Connection;
    use std::sync::Once;

    static INIT: Once = Once::new();

    fn test_connection() -> PgConnection {
        let url = std::env::var("TEST_DATABASE_URL")
            .unwrap_or_else(|_| "postgres://localhost/myapp_test".to_string());
        let mut conn = PgConnection::establish(&url)
            .expect("Failed to connect to test DB");

        // Run migrations once
        INIT.call_once(|| {
            run_migrations(&mut conn).expect("Migrations failed");
        });

        // Each test runs in a transaction that rolls back
        conn.begin_test_transaction()
            .expect("Failed to start test transaction");
        conn
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_create_user() {
        let mut conn = test_connection();
        seed_development_data(&mut conn).unwrap();

        let count: i64 = users::table
            .count()
            .get_result(&mut conn)
            .unwrap();
        assert!(count >= 3);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_orders_cascade_delete() {
        let mut conn = test_connection();
        // Insert user, insert order, delete user
        // Order should cascade-delete
        // ... test logic
    }
}
```

instruction: How do I implement connection pooling with Diesel using r2d2, including health checks, connection lifecycle hooks, and graceful shutdown?
output:
<think>
Diesel uses `r2d2` for connection pooling. The pool manages a set of database connections, handles checkout/checkin, and provides connection health validation. Key configuration: 1) `max_size` — maximum connections (match your DB's connection limit / number of app instances), 2) `min_idle` — keep this many connections ready, 3) `connection_timeout` — how long to wait for a connection, 4) `idle_timeout` — close connections idle longer than this, 5) `max_lifetime` — close connections older than this (prevents stale connections). For health checks, r2d2 can test connections on checkout (`test_on_check_out`). For custom lifecycle hooks, implement the `r2d2::CustomizeConnection` trait — this runs setup SQL on each new connection (e.g., SET schema, SET timezone). For metrics, r2d2 provides `pool.state()` with active/idle counts. Graceful shutdown: the pool is dropped when the application exits, which closes all connections. For explicit shutdown, drop the pool. I should also show the `deadpool` alternative for async contexts since r2d2 is sync-only.
</think>

Here's a production connection pooling setup with Diesel:

```rust
use diesel::prelude::*;
use diesel::pg::PgConnection;
use diesel::r2d2::{
    self, ConnectionManager, Pool, PooledConnection, CustomizeConnection,
};
use std::time::Duration;
use std::sync::Arc;
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, Ordering};

// === Pool configuration ===
#[derive(Clone)]
pub struct DbConfig {
    pub url: String,
    pub max_connections: u32,
    pub min_idle: u32,
    pub connection_timeout: Duration,
    pub idle_timeout: Duration,
    pub max_lifetime: Duration,
    pub default_schema: Option<String>,
}

impl Default for DbConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            url: std::env::var("DATABASE_URL")
                .unwrap_or_else(|_| "postgres://localhost/myapp".into()),
            max_connections: 20,
            min_idle: 5,
            connection_timeout: Duration::from_secs(5),
            idle_timeout: Duration::from_secs(300),
            max_lifetime: Duration::from_secs(1800),
            default_schema: None,
        }
    }
}

// === Connection customizer (lifecycle hooks) ===
#[derive(Debug)]
struct ConnectionCustomizer {
    schema: Option<String>,
}

impl CustomizeConnection<PgConnection, r2d2::Error> for ConnectionCustomizer {
    fn on_acquire(&self, conn: &mut PgConnection) -> Result<(), r2d2::Error> {
        // Runs when a new connection is created
        if let Some(ref schema) = self.schema {
            diesel::sql_query(format!("SET search_path TO {}, public", schema))
                .execute(conn)
                .map_err(|e| r2d2::Error::QueryError(e))?;
        }

        // Set statement timeout to prevent runaway queries
        diesel::sql_query("SET statement_timeout = '30s'")
            .execute(conn)
            .map_err(|e| r2d2::Error::QueryError(e))?;

        // Set application name for pg_stat_activity
        diesel::sql_query("SET application_name = 'myapp'")
            .execute(conn)
            .map_err(|e| r2d2::Error::QueryError(e))?;

        println!("New connection acquired");
        Ok(())
    }

    fn on_release(&self, _conn: PgConnection) {
        println!("Connection released back to pool");
    }
}

// === Pool wrapper with metrics and health ===
pub type DbPool = Pool<ConnectionManager<PgConnection>>;
pub type DbConn = PooledConnection<ConnectionManager<PgConnection>>;

pub struct Database {
    pool: DbPool,
    healthy: Arc<AtomicBool>,
}

impl Database {
    pub fn new(config: DbConfig) -> Result<Self, r2d2::PoolError> {
        let manager = ConnectionManager::<PgConnection>::new(&config.url);

        let customizer = ConnectionCustomizer {
            schema: config.default_schema.clone(),
        };

        let pool = Pool::builder()
            .max_size(config.max_connections)
            .min_idle(Some(config.min_idle))
            .connection_timeout(config.connection_timeout)
            .idle_timeout(Some(config.idle_timeout))
            .max_lifetime(Some(config.max_lifetime))
            .test_on_check_out(true)  // Validate connection on checkout
            .connection_customizer(Box::new(customizer))
            .build(manager)?;

        Ok(Self {
            pool,
            healthy: Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(true)),
        })
    }

    pub fn get_conn(&self) -> Result<DbConn, r2d2::PoolError> {
        self.pool.get()
    }

    pub fn health_check(&self) -> HealthStatus {
        let state = self.pool.state();

        // Try to get a connection
        let can_connect = match self.pool.get() {
            Ok(mut conn) => {
                diesel::sql_query("SELECT 1")
                    .execute(&mut *conn)
                    .is_ok()
            }
            Err(_) => false,
        };

        let healthy = can_connect && state.idle_connections > 0;
        self.healthy.store(healthy, Ordering::Relaxed);

        HealthStatus {
            healthy,
            total_connections: state.connections,
            idle_connections: state.idle_connections,
            in_use: state.connections - state.idle_connections,
            max_size: self.pool.max_size(),
        }
    }

    pub fn is_healthy(&self) -> bool {
        self.healthy.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
    }

    pub fn metrics(&self) -> PoolMetrics {
        let state = self.pool.state();
        PoolMetrics {
            total: state.connections,
            idle: state.idle_connections,
            active: state.connections - state.idle_connections,
            max: self.pool.max_size(),
            utilization: if self.pool.max_size() > 0 {
                (state.connections - state.idle_connections) as f64
                    / self.pool.max_size() as f64
            } else {
                0.0
            },
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, serde::Serialize)]
pub struct HealthStatus {
    pub healthy: bool,
    pub total_connections: u32,
    pub idle_connections: u32,
    pub in_use: u32,
    pub max_size: u32,
}

#[derive(Debug, serde::Serialize)]
pub struct PoolMetrics {
    pub total: u32,
    pub idle: u32,
    pub active: u32,
    pub max: u32,
    pub utilization: f64,
}

// === Usage with transactions ===
impl Database {
    pub fn transaction<T, F>(&self, f: F) -> Result<T, diesel::result::Error>
    where
        F: FnOnce(&mut PgConnection) -> Result<T, diesel::result::Error>,
    {
        let mut conn = self.get_conn()
            .map_err(|_| diesel::result::Error::DatabaseError(
                diesel::result::DatabaseErrorKind::Unknown,
                Box::new("Pool exhausted".to_string()),
            ))?;
        conn.transaction(f)
    }
}

// === Application setup ===
fn main() {
    let db = Database::new(DbConfig {
        url: "postgres://localhost/myapp".into(),
        max_connections: 20,
        min_idle: 5,
        ..Default::default()
    }).expect("Failed to create pool");

    // Health check
    let health = db.health_check();
    println!("DB Health: {:?}", health);

    // Transaction
    let result = db.transaction(|conn| {
        // Multiple queries in one transaction
        diesel::sql_query("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('test')")
            .execute(conn)?;
        Ok("done")
    });
}
```
